content.text: Beyond Data Parallelism: Why We Need Model Parallelism

Data parallelism (DDP/ZeRO) replicates the full model on every GPU and shards data. This breaks down when a single model replica exceeds GPU memory. A 70-billion-parameter model in BF16 requires ~140 GB — far beyond the 80 GB of a single A100 or H100. Two complementary strategies split the model itself across GPUs:
splits individual weight matrices across GPUs — each GPU holds a horizontal or vertical slice of each matrix and computes a partial result. An all-reduce combines shards after each layer.
assigns consecutive transformer layers to different GPUs — GPU 0 runs layers 1–8, GPU 1 runs layers 9–16, and so on. A micro-batch flows through GPUs in sequence like a factory pipeline.
These strategies compose with each other and with data parallelism into the
approach used by Megatron-LM and DeepSpeed to train trillion-parameter models.

attrs.alt: Tensor parallelism (weight matrix split across GPU A/B with all-reduce) and pipeline parallelism (layers on separate GPUs with micro-batch F/B schedule)

content.text: Tensor parallelism (Megatron-LM, Shoeybi et al. 2019) exploits the structure of Transformer MLP and attention layers. An MLP layer computes
. A single all-reduce sums the results:
. This halves the memory footprint per GPU for the weight matrices with only one synchronisation point per MLP.

# Megatron-style tensor parallelism for a two-layer MLP
# Assumes torch.distributed is initialised with a TP process group

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.distributed as dist

class ColumnParallelLinear(nn.Module):
    """Split weight column-wise: each rank holds (in_features, out_features/tp_size)."""
    def __init__(self, in_features: int, out_features: int, tp_group):
        super().__init__()
        self.tp_group = tp_group
        tp_size = dist.get_world_size(group=tp_group)
        assert out_features % tp_size == 0
        self.local_out = out_features // tp_size
        self.weight = nn.Parameter(torch.empty(self.local_out, in_features))
        nn.init.normal_(self.weight, std=0.02)

    def forward(self, x):
        # Each rank computes its own shard of the output
        return torch.nn.functional.linear(x, self.weight)


class RowParallelLinear(nn.Module):
    """Split weight row-wise: each rank holds (out_features, in_features/tp_size)."""
    def __init__(self, in_features: int, out_features: int, tp_group):
        super().__init__()
        self.tp_group = tp_group
        tp_size = dist.get_world_size(group=tp_group)
        assert in_features % tp_size == 0
        self.local_in = in_features // tp_size
        self.weight = nn.Parameter(torch.empty(out_features, self.local_in))
        nn.init.normal_(self.weight, std=0.02)

    def forward(self, x):
        # Compute partial output then all-reduce across TP group
        partial = torch.nn.functional.linear(x, self.weight)
        dist.all_reduce(partial, op=dist.ReduceOp.SUM, group=self.tp_group)
        return partial


class TensorParallelMLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, hidden: int, ffn: int, tp_group):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = ColumnParallelLinear(hidden, ffn, tp_group)
        self.fc2 = RowParallelLinear(ffn, hidden, tp_group)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(torch.nn.functional.gelu(self.fc1(x)))
Multi-head attention splits naturally across the head dimension. With
attention heads independently — no communication during the attention computation. The projection matrix after attention is row-split, requiring one all-reduce per layer, identical to the MLP case.
requires 2 all-reduces per transformer layer (one after attention, one after MLP). Each all-reduce transfers

of the activation size. On NVLink (600 GB/s bidirectional), this is negligible within a node. Across nodes over InfiniBand (200–400 Gbps), TP becomes a bottleneck and is typically restricted to intra-node ranks.
Pipeline parallelism (Huang et al. 2019 — GPipe) assigns consecutive groups of transformer layers to different GPUs. With
pipeline stages (one GPU per stage) and
layers. A mini-batch is split into
micro-batches that flow through the pipeline.
micro-batches forward through the pipeline before computing any backward passes. This simplifies gradient computation but creates a
— idle time while the pipeline fills and drains:
micro-batches, bubble fraction = 3/11 ≈ 27%. Increasing micro-batches reduces the bubble: at

The 1F1B (one-forward-one-backward) schedule, used in Megatron-LM, interleaves forward and backward micro-batches to keep all stages busy. Once the pipeline is full, each stage alternates: process one forward micro-batch, then immediately process one backward micro-batch. This cuts the bubble fraction roughly in half compared to GPipe while keeping peak memory roughly constant.

# Simplified pipeline parallelism with micro-batch scheduling
# Each rank runs one pipeline stage (group of consecutive layers)

import torch
import torch.distributed as dist
from typing import Optional

class PipelineStage(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, layers: torch.nn.ModuleList,
                 rank: int, world_size: int,
                 pp_group):
        super().__init__()
        self.layers = layers
        self.rank = rank
        self.world_size = world_size
        self.pp_group = pp_group
        self.is_first = rank == 0
        self.is_last  = rank == world_size - 1

    def forward(self, x: Optional[torch.Tensor]) -> Optional[torch.Tensor]:
        # Receive activation from previous stage (except first stage)
        if not self.is_first:
            shape = [s for s in x.shape]  # x is a shape tensor here (simplified)
            act = torch.empty(shape, device='cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
            dist.recv(act, src=self.rank - 1, group=self.pp_group)
            x = act

        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)

        # Send activation to next stage (except last stage)
        if not self.is_last:
            dist.send(x.detach(), dst=self.rank + 1, group=self.pp_group)
            return None
        return x  # last stage returns logits


def run_pipeline_microbatches(stage: PipelineStage,
                               micro_batches: list[torch.Tensor],
                               optimizer: torch.optim.Optimizer,
                               criterion) -> float:
    """
    GPipe-style: all forward passes, then all backward passes.
    Returns loss on last stage (rank == world_size-1).
    """
    activations = []
    total_loss = 0.0

    # Forward pass over all micro-batches
    for mb in micro_batches:
        out = stage(mb)
        if out is not None:  # last stage
            loss = criterion(out) / len(micro_batches)
            total_loss += loss.item()
            activations.append(loss)
        else:
            activations.append(None)

    # Backward pass over all micro-batches
    for act in activations:
        if act is not None:
            act.backward()

    optimizer.step()
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    return total_loss
Each pipeline stage must store intermediate activations for the backward pass. With
micro-batches in flight simultaneously, peak activation memory scales as

. Activation recomputation (also called gradient checkpointing) discards activations after the forward pass and recomputes them from the saved inputs during the backward pass. This trades ~33% extra compute for a roughly
Megatron-LM (NVIDIA, 2019–2024) is the canonical open-source implementation of tensor and pipeline parallelism for Transformer models. It trains GPT, BERT, T5, and LLaMA variants at scale.

# Launching Megatron-LM with 3D parallelism
# TP=4 (within node), PP=4 (across nodes), DP=8 (data parallel replicas)
# Total GPUs = 4 * 4 * 8 = 128

torchrun \
    --nproc_per_node=8 \
    --nnodes=16 \
    pretrain_gpt.py \
    --tensor-model-parallel-size 4 \
    --pipeline-model-parallel-size 4 \
    --num-layers 96 \
    --hidden-size 12288 \
    --num-attention-heads 96 \
    --seq-length 2048 \
    --global-batch-size 1024 \
    --micro-batch-size 1 \
    --train-iters 500000
Megatron-LM handles the TP/PP process group setup automatically from the
flags. The DP degree is inferred: DP = world_size / (TP × PP).
With world size = TP × PP × DP, ranks are assigned as follows:
At scale (hundreds to thousands of GPUs), data parallelism alone cannot fit the model, and tensor parallelism alone cannot scale beyond NVLink bandwidth. The solution is
A 530-billion-parameter Megatron model (MT-NLG, 2021) used TP=8, PP=35, DP=12 on 280 A100 nodes (2240 GPUs total). A 1-trillion-parameter model would require PP or ZeRO-3 in addition.

Set TP to the number of GPUs per node (typically 8) to stay on NVLink. Set PP to span enough nodes to fit the model. Set DP to fill the remaining available GPUs. Micro-batch size should satisfy: m ≥ 4(p−1) to keep bubble fraction under 20%.

PyTorch's FSDP (Wright et al. 2022) is an alternative to manual tensor parallelism that shards model parameters, gradients, and optimiser states across the DP group — the same idea as ZeRO Stage 3. Unlike Megatron-LM, FSDP requires no model-code changes:

from torch.distributed.fsdp import FullyShardedDataParallel as FSDP
from torch.distributed.fsdp import MixedPrecision, ShardingStrategy
import torch

# Wrap existing model — no architecture changes required
model = FSDP(
    YourTransformerModel(),
    sharding_strategy=ShardingStrategy.FULL_SHARD,   # ZeRO-3 equivalent
    mixed_precision=MixedPrecision(
        param_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
        reduce_dtype=torch.float32,
        buffer_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    ),
    device_id=torch.cuda.current_device(),
)

# Training loop is unchanged — FSDP handles all-gathers before forward,
# reduce-scatters after backward, and shard storage in between.
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)
for x, y in dataloader:
    loss = model(x, y)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    optimizer.zero_grad()
GPUs. Before each forward/backward, FSDP issues an all-gather to reconstruct the full weight matrix, then immediately discards the non-local shards. This reduces peak memory from
at the cost of 3× the parameter data volume in communication per layer (gather for forward, gather for backward, reduce-scatter for gradient).

Tensor parallelism splits weight matrices column-then-row across GPUs, requiring one all-reduce per layer and keeping communication within NVLink bandwidth. Pipeline parallelism splits consecutive layers across GPUs, amortising bubble overhead with many micro-batches and 1F1B scheduling. 3D parallelism (DP + TP + PP) is the production approach for models exceeding single-node memory, used in Megatron-LM and DeepSpeed to train trillion-parameter models. FSDP provides ZeRO-3-style parameter sharding without model-code changes, making it practical for most research settings.
column/row split of weight matrices; 2 all-reduces per layer; restrict to intra-node (NVLink) GPUs.
layer groups on separate GPUs; bubble fraction = (p−1)/(m+p−1); 1F1B halves bubble vs GPipe.
TP × PP × DP = total GPU count; Megatron-LM is the reference implementation.
drop-in ZeRO-3 for any PyTorch model; trades 3× communication per layer for O(P/N) memory.
